name: Care plan writing & reviews
description: Drafting, updating, reviewing resident care plans
current_process: In a typical UK care home, care plan writing and reviews are largely manual processes. A registered manager or senior carer assesses the resident (covering physical, emotional, social, and clinical needs), then drafts a person-centred care plan using either paper templates or basic digital care planning software. Each care plan must cover every aspect of the service user's life within the care setting. Reviews are triggered by: scheduled intervals (typically monthly or quarterly), major events (falls, hospital admissions, medication changes), or CQC inspection preparation. During a review, the manager re-reads the existing care plan, cross-references daily notes and incident reports, consults with care staff and family members, updates goals and interventions, and documents changes. The process is often done at a desk, away from the resident, despite CQC guidance discouraging remote care planning. Paper-based homes keep physical folders that must be manually updated across multiple copies.
time_spent: KareInn reports saving 90 minutes per care plan with their digital system, implying manual care plans take significantly longer. Quik AI (Carey) reports managers saving ~5 hours per week and senior carers saving ~1 hour per day through AI-assisted plan review. CareCallAI claims care plans can be created 'in minutes' with AI versus hours manually. StoriiCare claims 'up to 6 hours per week per staff member' saved. Industry-wide, nearly half (48%) of care managers say paperwork prevents them from doing the job they should be doing (Nursing in Practice survey). A registered manager in a 40-bed home may spend 8–15 hours per week on care plan writing and review activities across all residents. [uncertain] Exact hours vary significantly by home size, staff ratios, and whether digital systems are already in use.
ai_solution: AI can address care plan writing and reviews in several ways: (1) Automated drafting — AI generates person-centred care plans from assessment data, clinical observations, and daily logs, which managers then review, edit, and approve (CareCallAI, StoriiCare AI Care Planning). (2) Intelligent review — AI analyses existing care plans against daily notes, incident reports, and clinical data to flag gaps, contradictions, or outdated information, ensuring plans reflect current needs (Quik AI Carey). (3) Compliance checking — AI cross-references care plans against CQC quality statements and organisational policies to identify compliance gaps before inspection (Carey reports 43% to 85% compliance improvement). (4) NLP summarisation — natural language processing analyses progress notes, pinpoints essential information, and surfaces key details for review. (5) Risk flagging — AI identifies medication interactions, falls patterns, weight changes, and other clinical risks from aggregated data. (6) Template generation — AI suggests appropriate assessment templates (Waterlow, MUST, NEWS2) based on resident profile and auto-calculates scores.
buyer_language: "I spend my evenings writing care plans instead of being on the floor with my residents." "Every time someone has a fall or comes back from hospital, I'm rewriting the whole plan from scratch." "CQC want to see person-centred care plans but I don't have the time to make each one truly personal." "I've got 40 residents and I'm supposed to review every care plan every month — it's impossible." "My senior carers are brilliant at caring but they struggle with the writing side." "I dread inspection because I know some of the care plans are out of date."
outcome_statement: Care plans drafted in minutes instead of hours, with AI pulling in assessment data, daily observations, and clinical notes automatically. Monthly reviews flagged and pre-populated so managers approve changes rather than starting from scratch. Compliance gaps identified before CQC inspection, not during it.
regulatory_alignment: Care plan writing and reviews map to multiple CQC quality statements under the Single Assessment Framework: (1) Safe — 'Assessing risk, safety monitoring and management' and 'Safeguarding'; (2) Effective — 'Assessing needs' and 'Delivering evidence-based care and treatment'; (3) Responsive — 'Person-centred care' and 'Care provision, integration and continuity'; (4) Well-led — 'Governance, management and sustainability' and 'Learning, improvement and innovation.' Under the previous KLOE framework, care plans were examined under Safe (S1, S2), Effective (E1, E2, E3), Responsive (R1, R2), and Well-led (W1, W3). CQC explicitly requires full involvement of service users in care planning and discourages planning done remote from the individual.
existing_tech_gap: Current care software (e.g., Person Centred Software, Access Care Planning, PASS) provides digital templates and storage for care plans but does not draft content, flag review needs intelligently, or cross-reference daily notes against care plan accuracy. The gap is between digitisation (moving paper to screen) and intelligence (making the content smarter). AI adds unique value by: generating draft text from structured data, identifying when a care plan is out of date based on real-world events, cross-referencing compliance requirements automatically, and summarising daily notes into actionable care plan updates. Existing digital care planning tools are essentially electronic filing cabinets — AI turns them into active clinical decision support.
implementation_complexity: Plug-and-play if using an AI overlay like Quik AI Carey (integrates with existing care software without replacement). Requires integration if building AI into an existing care planning system (API connections to care records, clinical data, daily logs). May require some process redesign for staff workflow — moving from 'write the whole plan' to 'review and approve AI draft.' Staff training needed on reviewing AI-generated content rather than writing from scratch. Typically operational within days to weeks rather than months.
proof_of_concept_availability: Yes — multiple working examples exist. Quik AI (Carey) has published case studies showing 43% to 85% compliance improvement across 12,007 audits. CareCallAI offers a live 30-day free trial with AI care plan drafting. StoriiCare offers AI Care Planning as a product feature. KareInn reports 90-minute time savings per care plan. The workflow is well-proven and multiple vendors offer demos or trials.
